body.text: The Tremor Discharge. Stand up and shake your arms and legs vigorously for 30 seconds. Imagine shaking the 'static' off your skin.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using neurogenic-style shaking to discharge excessive sympathetic charge and reset muscle spindles.

mind.text: The Top 3 Triage. Write down exactly 3 urgent tasks on a scrap of paper, then physically put the pen in another room.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to prioritize and stop the 'infinite loop' of cognitive processing.

social.text: Declare the Limit. Tell a safe person: 'I am at my limit. I need 10 minutes of silence to process.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using verbal boundary setting to reduce the 'social tax' while in high-arousal overload.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like a checklist of demands. Goal: Creating a Sanctuary.

body.text: Auditory Shielding. Put on noise-canceling headphones or earplugs to shut out the environmental 'noise'.
body.physioTip: Best for: Reducing the total amount of incoming data to allow the brain's 'switchboard' to cool down.

mind.text: The Foot-to-Floor Anchor. Focus entirely on the sensation of your feet inside your shoes and the pressure of the floor for one minute.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Moving focus from the high-speed 'head' energy to the stable, low-speed 'base' of the body.

social.text: The Question Embargo. Explicitly tell those around you: 'I am on a no-ask zone for the next hour. No questions unless it's an emergency.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Proactively stopping incoming social demands to prevent a high-arousal 'snap' or meltdown.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Twitchy
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel twitchy. You have an enormous amount of 'mobilization' energy ready to go, but nowhere to point it. You are like a racehorse behind the gate.
sensationData.blueprint: Unchanneled Sympathetic Energy. Your body has dumped adrenaline into your system to prepare for action, but since you aren't fighting or fleeing, the energy is vibrating in your muscles.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Restless legs; bouncing knees; tapping fingers; and a feeling of 'electricity' running under the skin.
sensationData.metaphor: A live wire that has fallen on the ground—sparking and jumping with energy that has no grounded destination.
sensationData.theImpulse: To pace the room, to bite your nails, to check your phone every 5 seconds, and to move without purpose.
sensationData.superpower: Ready-For-Action. You are primed. If a real emergency happened right now, you would be the first person to move.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'buzz' is making you feel anxious internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Entrainment Braking. Match your finger tapping to an external rhythm (like a clock or a steady beat), then gradually and consciously slow the tap down over 30 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'entrainment' to manually down-shift the nervous system by anchoring it to an external pace, pulling focus out of the internal jitter.

mind.text: Micro-Sorting. Find 10 small objects nearby (coins, pens, paperclips). Sort them by a specific rule: size, color, or weight. Focus entirely on the objects.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Channeling 'jittery' fine motor energy into an analytical external task to re-engage the prefrontal cortex and the environment.

social.text: The GIF Dispatch. Text a safe person a funny or high-energy GIF. Use the digital interaction to 'export' the jittery spark into a social connection.
social.physioTip: Best for: Converting unchanneled physical energy into a brief social 'ping,' which provides an external destination for the 'Live Wire' energy.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too slow or annoying. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Burst Discharge. Perform 15 rapid squats or a quick set of push-ups against the wall. Give your large muscles a high-intensity job to do right now.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing a rapid metabolic 'sink' for excess adrenaline. Large muscle recruitment pulls the 'jitter' back into the skeletal core to be burned off.

mind.text: The Internal Metronome. Tap a steady beat on your own thigh. Count out loud: 1-2-3-4, matching the tap to your voice. Feel the touch on your skin.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using the prefrontal cortex to impose structure on chaotic motor energy, anchoring the brain back into the physical rhythm of the body.

social.text: The Percussive Hum. Hum a low, steady note with enough volume to feel your chest and throat vibrate. Focus entirely on the internal vibration.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using vocal cord vibration to stimulate the vagus nerve while providing an internal sensory anchor to 'ground' the twitchy electricity.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Surging
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel a surge. Something unexpected just happened, and your system has 'spiked' its energy to meet the new information. You are in a state of high-alert appraisal.
sensationData.blueprint: Acute Orientation Response. Your system is rapidly shifting gears to assess if a new stimulus is a threat or an opportunity, resulting in a sudden sympathetic surge.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A sudden 'gasp' or sharp inhale; wide eyes; a cold chill running down the spine; and a momentary 'freeze' followed by a racing heart.
sensationData.metaphor: A power surge in a building during a lightning storm—the lights flicker brightly for a second before the breakers reset.
sensationData.theImpulse: To gasp, to stare, to ask 'What was that?', and to feel a sudden prickle of cold or heat.
sensationData.superpower: Instant Adaptation. Your system just gave you a massive boost of awareness so you can survive the next 5 seconds, no matter what happens.
sensationData.generalGoal: Stabilizing the current. Slowing the return.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The internal 'spike' feels overwhelming. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Panoramic Scan. Slowly rotate your head to the far left and then slowly to the far right. Take in the full 180-degree view of the room's corners.
body.physioTip: Best for: Activating cervical proprioceptors and the vestibular system to signal the brainstem that the external environment is currently stable.

mind.text: The Constant Hunt. Identify 3 large things in the room that have NOT changed in the last minute (e.g., the door, the floor, the heavy table).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a 'temporal anchor' to stabilize a brain that feels fragmented by a sudden change, grounding it in external continuity.

social.text: The Fact Check. Say out loud in a clear voice: 'That was a surprise, but the room is still here and I am safe.' Listen to your own voice.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'Thinking Brain' to override the survival brain's alarm, externalizing the logic to re-orient the prefrontal cortex.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels startling or sharp. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Resistance Exhale. Inhale for 3 seconds, then exhale through pursed lips (like blowing through a straw) for a long count of 8. Feel the pressure.
body.physioTip: Best for: Creating positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) to mechanically stimulate the vagus nerve and pull the heart rate back into the body's midline.

mind.text: The Solid Tether. Reach out and place your hand flat against a heavy, immovable object (a wall or desk). Squeeze slightly and feel the resistance in your hand.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using 'closed-chain' tactile input to pull a spaced-out brain back into the physical container of the arm and palm.

social.text: Diaphragm Anchor. Place one hand firmly on your belly and the other on your heart. Wait until you can feel the warmth of your hands on your skin.
social.physioTip: Best for: Re-establishing a connection with the 'Self' after a shock has ejected focus to the periphery, providing a safe, internal embrace.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Burning
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel like you are burning. Your system is radiating high-voltage energy because it's trying to incinerate a perceived threat or injustice. You aren't 'toxic'; you are protective.
sensationData.blueprint: Thermal sympathetic activation. Your metabolic rate has spiked to generate heat and power, preparing your body for a high-intensity defense.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Internal heat rising toward the face; dry mouth; a prickling sensation in the skin; and eyes that feel sharp or 'stinging'.
sensationData.metaphor: A furnace with the door wide open. The heat is useful for forging, but it consumes everything if it's not directed.
sensationData.theImpulse: To scorch others with words, to move fast and hard, and to find an immediate outlet for the internal fire.
sensationData.superpower: Pure Vitality. You have the energy of a star. When cooled slightly, this is the power of absolute conviction and boundary setting.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The fire is consuming you from within. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Radial Cooling. Run cold water over your inner wrists for 60 seconds. Focus entirely on the sensation of the cold liquid against your pulse points.
body.physioTip: Best for: Cooling the blood at high-circulation points where the radial artery is superficial, helping to lower systemic core temperature and signal safety.

mind.text: The Cold Search. Find one object in the room that is made of metal, stone, or glass. Look at it and imagine its temperature against your skin.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting the brain from 'hot' emotional triggers to cold environmental data, utilizing visual-thermal association to dampen arousal.

social.text: Thermal Alert. Text a safe person or say out loud: 'Running hot right now. Need a moment of cool silence.' Then move to a cooler physical space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using social agency to change your immediate thermal environment, forcing the body’s sensors to pivot toward homeostatic cooling.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment is adding fuel to your fire. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Sitali Cooling. Curl your tongue into a 'U' shape (or purse your lips) and inhale slowly through the mouth. Feel the cold air hitting the moisture on your tongue.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing evaporative cooling on the tongue to send an immediate 'temperature drop' signal to the brainstem through the glossopharyngeal nerve.

mind.text: The Blue Mist. Close your eyes. Imagine a cool, blue mist entering your lungs with every inhale, neutralizing the 'red' heat in your chest and belly.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visceral imagery to facilitate systemic thermal settling, pulling the focus back to the internal process of regulation.

social.text: Internal Thermal Pivot. Visualize yourself stepping into a cold, quiet forest or a snow-covered field. Feel the silence and the cold on your face.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing a 'safe-place' mental image to simulate an environment that requires a lower metabolic output, pulling you back into a contained state.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Boiling
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you are boiling. You are at the tipping point where liquid energy turns into steam pressure. Your system is ready to erupt to keep you from being suppressed.
sensationData.blueprint: High-pressure sympathetic surge. Your blood pressure and heart rate are at peak levels, creating a feeling of internal turbulence.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Churning in the stomach; a 'bubbling' feeling in the chest; and a vibrating quality to your limbs and jaw.
sensationData.metaphor: A pot on the stove with the lid rattling. The energy is contained, but only just barely.
sensationData.theImpulse: To boil over, to snap, to hiss, and to release the pressure through an explosive outburst.
sensationData.superpower: Transformative Force. Steam moves engines. This boiling energy is the precursor to massive change if the 'steam' is channeled through a pipe.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The pressure is shaking you from the inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Weighted Pace. Pace the room with heavy, rhythmic steps. As you walk, count the tiles or floorboards out loud: 1, 2, 3, 4.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rhythmic bone-loading and vocalization to anchor the brain in the external physical environment, pulling awareness away from the internal churn.

mind.text: Temporal Locking. Look at a clock with a second hand (or a digital timer). Match your finger-tapping to the steady 'tick' for 30 seconds.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Outsourcing your internal 'tempo' to a steady external source to manually override a racing, 'boiling' internal pulse.

social.text: The Core 'Why'. Say out loud to the room: 'I am furious because [X] is important to me.' Labeling the value makes the anger objective and external.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the prefrontal cortex to label the values behind the heat, which moves the energy from 'reactive boiling' to 'principled conviction'.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Everything feels like a trigger. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Vagal Toning (VOO). Inhale deeply and make a low, vibrating 'Vooooo' sound on the exhale. Focus entirely on the vibration in your chest and throat.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using internal sound vibration to stimulate the vagus nerve branch in the throat, providing a mechanical 'internal vent' for high pressure.

mind.text: The Steam Valve. Close your eyes. Imagine a safe pressure valve on top of your head slowly opening. Visualize the hot steam leaving your body through the valve.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using ideokinesis to facilitate a visceral release of the pressure, pulling the focus back to the internal sensation of cooling.

social.text: Impact Discharge. Press your hands together as hard as possible (the 'prayer' position) and feel the resistance of your own palms.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using a brief, high-intensity isometric impact to discharge motor energy within your own frame, re-establishing your physical boundaries.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Clenched
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you are clenched. Your system has hardened your exterior to protect your vulnerability. You are a fortress under siege.
sensationData.blueprint: Protective Armoring. High sympathetic arousal is causing sustained muscular contraction (bracing) to protect your joints and organs from impact.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Tight jaw; shoulders up to the ears; fists balled; and a feeling of 'holding on' for dear life.
sensationData.metaphor: A fist that has forgotten how to open. Strong, but unable to feel or receive.
sensationData.theImpulse: To hold tighter, to refuse help, to grit your teeth, and to muscle through the pain.
sensationData.superpower: Unshakeable Strength. In this state, you are an anchor. Nothing can move you or break you while you are braced.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You are a prisoner inside your own tension. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Pliability Scan. Identify three objects in your immediate view that are physically 'soft', 'malleable', or 'flexible' (e.g., a cushion, a plant, a curtain).
body.physioTip: Best for: Priming the brain with external visual cues of 'non-rigidity' to counteract the internal 'armor' bias and pull awareness into the room.

mind.text: The Un-Armoring. Visualize yourself stepping out of a heavy, metal suit of armor in this exact room. See each piece land on the floor away from you.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using symbolic imagery to signal to the nervous system that the 'siege' is over and the environment is safe enough to soften.

social.text: Pressure Matching. If with a safe person, ask for a firm hand-squeeze. Match their pressure exactly for 5 seconds, then let go of the grip together.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using co-regulated resistance to satisfy the body's urge to 'hold' while tethering that effort to a safe external human anchor.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels like a physical threat. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Full-Body Flush (PIR). Squeeze every muscle in your body (fists, jaw, toes) even tighter for 5 seconds. On a long exhale, drop the tension all at once.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing Post-Isometric Relaxation (PIR) to bypass the brain's 'guarding' reflex, pulling awareness into the muscles to force a systemic release.

mind.text: The Knot Search. Close your eyes and locate the absolute 'center' of the clench in your body. Observe its shape and density from the inside.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'being' the tension to 'observing' the tension, which re-engages the prefrontal cortex with your internal state.

social.text: TMJ Release. Place your own fingertips on the hinges of your jaw. Apply gentle pressure while silently saying: 'It is safe to let the guard down.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Deactivating the primary cranial 'clench' trigger point, which helps pull the spaced-out system back into a regulated internal baseline.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Braced
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel braced. You are expecting an impact, so your system is pre-loading your muscles to absorb the blow.
sensationData.blueprint: Preparatory Sympathetic Load. Your brain is anticipating a challenge and has 'tightened the strings' of your system to ensure stability.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Lower back tension; knees locked; holding the breath; and a feeling of 'readiness' that is starting to ache.
sensationData.metaphor: A bridge under a heavy load—straining against the weight, but holding firm.
sensationData.theImpulse: To stay in one position, to monitor every sound, and to prepare for the worst.
sensationData.superpower: Durability. You are built to endure. Your system is currently maximizing its defensive architecture to keep you upright.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your internal structure is under strain. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Horizontal Scan. Identify three long, horizontal lines in the room (a shelf, a floorboard, a window frame). Follow each one slowly from end to end.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'visual horizons' to stabilize spatial orientation, which signals the brain to lower the high-arousal 'guarding' response.

mind.text: The Safe Container. Imagine yourself inside a solid, transparent dome. It is an impenetrable barrier that keeps all external pressure away from your skin.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a symbolic visual boundary to reduce the 'startle' reflex and the neurological need for constant muscular armor.

social.text: The Safety Ping. Text a trusted person: 'In bracing mode. Just sending a ping to stay connected.' Observe the phone as a physical link to the outside world.
social.physioTip: Best for: Outsourcing the search for safety to a reliable social tether, which pulls focus out of the internal bracing and toward the environment.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels unpredictable. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Spinal Unload. Place your hands on a table or chair. Push down firmly to let your spine grow tall, then slowly soften your elbows and feel your weight settle.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using 'closed-chain' resistance to re-align the vertebrae and then testing the system's ability to yield as the 'bracing' is consciously removed.

mind.text: The Lateral Shift. Slowly shift your weight from your left foot to your right. Focus entirely on the changing pressure in your heels and the soles of your feet.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Unlocking the knees and engaging the vestibular system to pull awareness back into the vertical axis and the support of the ground.

social.text: Wall Surrender. Lean your back fully against a solid wall. Feel the contact points from your sacrum to your shoulder blades. Let the wall 'carry' your spine.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing large-surface tactile contact to provide the nervous system with undeniable proof of external support, allowing the core to inhabit the body.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Racing
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that everything feels like it's racing. Your system just had a massive intake of information and is trying to process it at 100mph to catch up.
sensationData.blueprint: Tachy-psychia (Time Distortion). High adrenaline is making your brain process the environment faster than normal, making the world seem like it's in fast-forward.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Fluttering in the chest; eyes darting; thoughts jumping from topic to topic; and cold sweat.
sensationData.metaphor: A film being played at double speed—you can see everything, but you can't feel the meaning of anything.
sensationData.theImpulse: To talk fast, to move erratically, and to 'do everything' at once to stop the racing feeling.
sensationData.superpower: Rapid Integration. You are a high-speed processor. You can see connections and details that others miss because your 'shutter speed' is so high.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your internal thoughts are a blur. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Honey-Motion Orienting. Rotate your head to look around the room as if you were moving through thick honey. Move as slowly as possible, noticing every detail.
body.physioTip: Best for: Manually slowing the vestibular-ocular reflex to 'reset' the brain's frame-rate and reduce the internal sense of racing.

mind.text: High-Definition Description. Pick one external object. Describe its color and texture to yourself in extreme detail (e.g., 'A matte, dusty navy with a slight metallic sheen').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to switch from high-speed 'threat scanning' to slow 'analytical inspection,' pulling awareness into the room.

social.text: Slow-Talk Request. If with a safe person, ask them: 'Could you tell me a story or a recipe very slowly?' Focus entirely on the gaps between their words.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'social entrainment'—hitching your internal speed to someone else's slower vocal rhythm to pull you out of the mental blur.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world looks like a blur or a movie. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Parasympathetic Brake (4-7-8). Inhale through the nose for 4, hold for 7, and exhale through pursed lips for a slow count of 8. Feel your ribs move.
body.physioTip: Best for: Mechanically stimulating the vagus nerve to lower the heart rate, forcing the brain to re-occupy the biological midline.

mind.text: The Pulse Sync. Find your pulse at your wrist or neck. Count it silently for 30 seconds. Focus on the physical 'thump' against your finger.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a concrete, biological 'metronome' to anchor a racing mind back into the physical reality of the heart.

social.text: Thermal Grounding. Place your palms flat on a cool, solid surface (like a table or tile). Squeeze slightly and feel the cold against your skin.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using constant thermal data to 'ground' excess sympathetic energy, pulling awareness back into the hands and core.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Volatile
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel volatile. Your system is highly reactive because it detects a lack of safety. You are like a sensitive alarm system currently set to its highest trigger level.
sensationData.blueprint: Sympathetic Hyper-reactivity. Your nervous system is primed for a 'snap' response. The brain is scanning for any further threat to justify an immediate explosion.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Hot, shallow breathing; a tight, restless feeling in the chest; and eyes that feel like they are darting or scanning the room rapidly.
sensationData.metaphor: Dry brush in a drought. One small spark could start a fire, so your system is bracing for the flame.
sensationData.theImpulse: To react sharply to small annoyances, to pace, and to use 'biting' words to create distance.
sensationData.superpower: Rapid Response. You are incredibly quick right now. If directed toward a task, you could complete it in record time.
sensationData.generalGoal: Lowering the sensitivity. Creating a buffer.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel like you're 'fuming' internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Sensory Pivot. Physically leave the room you are in. Move to a space with different lighting, a different temperature, or step outside.
body.physioTip: Best for: Forcing a total reset of the 'orienting response' by changing the entire data feed, pulling awareness out of the internal 'fume' loop.

mind.text: The 3-3-3 Grounding. Name 3 things you see in the room and 3 distinct sounds you hear. Then move 3 small, separate parts of your body (e.g., big toe, pinky, tongue).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to cycle through multiple external sensory channels, which disrupts the hyper-fixated 'threat scan' typical of volatility.

social.text: The Extinguisher Signal. Text a safe person a 'fire extinguisher' emoji 🧯. If alone, say out loud to the room: 'I am running hot, I need this space to be quiet.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Establishing a social boundary with zero verbal tax, externalizing the need for a 'buffer' before an explosive interaction occurs.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels irritating and abrasive. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Vagal Splash. Splash cool water on your face, focusing on the area around your eyes, or hold a cold compress to the back of your neck. Focus on the temperature shock.
body.physioTip: Best for: Triggering the 'Mammalian Dive Reflex' via the trigeminal nerve to rapidly lower heart rate and pull the system back into a regulated internal baseline.

mind.text: The Resistance Exhale. Inhale through your nose for 3 seconds, then exhale through very small, pursed lips for 6 seconds. Feel the pressure in your chest.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using positive expiratory pressure to stimulate the vagus nerve and mechanically override the sympathetic surge from within the body.

social.text: Label the Spark. Identify the specific thing currently irritating you. Say silently: '[X] is the spark, but I am the one choosing to watch it burn.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Creating 'cognitive distance' by labeling the trigger, which shifts the brain from the reactive amygdala to the analytical prefrontal cortex.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Trembling
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you are trembling. This is your body's natural way of trying to 'shake off' or discharge trapped survival energy. It’s a sign that your system is trying to return to balance.
sensationData.blueprint: Neurogenic Tremoring. Your sympathetic system is attempting to release the high-voltage charge of a 'fright' response. It is a biological 'reset' button.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Involuntary shivering or shaking in the hands, legs, or jaw; chattering teeth; and a 'fluttering' sensation in the stomach.
sensationData.metaphor: A small bird ruffling its feathers after a close call with a predator—shaking to let the fear fall away.
sensationData.theImpulse: To hide the shaking, to tighten your muscles to stop the movement, or to feel 'out of control'.
sensationData.superpower: The Great Release. Your body is doing the hard work for you. Shaking is the most direct path back to the Green Zone.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The shaking feels scary from the inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Supported Discharge. Lean your back or hands firmly against a solid wall. Allow the shaking to continue while focusing on the cold, unmoving support of the bricks or wood.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing an external 'immovable' reference point so the brain feels safe enough to allow the neurogenic tremor to complete its discharge cycle without panic.

mind.text: The Calm Witness. Visualize a safe person or pet sitting in the room with you, watching the shaking with steady, kind, and non-judgmental eyes.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from internal 'alarm' to an external 'witness' perspective, reducing the shame-response that often blocks the discharge.

social.text: The Steady Anchor. If someone safe is nearby, ask them to place a steady, unmoving hand firmly on your shoulder. Focus on the stillness of their hand.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using an external human anchor to provide a 'still' point of reference, helping the brain distinguish between the shaking self and the stable world.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: You feel detached or 'spaced out' from the trembling. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Somatic Containment. Wrap yourself tightly in a blanket or give yourself a firm 'self-hug' (hands crossing to opposite shoulders). Squeeze until you feel your ribs.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using deep pressure (DTP) to define the body's 'envelope,' helping the brain re-tether to the physical midline during the tremor.

mind.text: Tremor Tracking. Close your eyes. Observe the rhythm of the tremors for 10 seconds. Is it fast or slow? Locate the 'epicenter'—is it in your jaw, chest, or legs?
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from a state of 'being shaken' to a state of 'observing movement,' which re-engages the prefrontal cortex with the internal body.

social.text: Frequency Matching (VOO). Inhale and make a low, vibrating 'Vooooo' sound on the exhale. Try to match the internal vibration of your voice to the tremor in your body.
social.physioTip: Best for: Harmonizing the 'fright' vibration with an intentional vocal vibration, signaling the brain that the internal movement is organized and intentional.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Pressurized
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel pressurized. You are holding a lot of 'mobilization' fuel inside, but the 'outlets' feel blocked. You are a container of high-potential energy.
sensationData.blueprint: Inhibited Sympathetic Drive. Adrenaline is high, but your 'social engagement' system is trying to keep a lid on it, creating an internal pressure cooker effect.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Pressure behind the eyes; a tight jaw; and a feeling like your skin is 'too tight' for your body.
sensationData.metaphor: A garden hose with a kink in it. The water is trying to flow, but the pressure is building up at the blockage.
sensationData.theImpulse: To snap, to grit your teeth, and to find something—anything—to push against.
sensationData.superpower: Internal Drive. This pressure is the 'fuel' of change. If you can point it at a task, you will be unstoppable.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The pressure feels like it's building in your head. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Horizon Scan. Look as far away as possible—out a window or down a long hallway. Follow the furthest horizontal line you can see with your eyes.
body.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'threat-tunnel vision' by expanding the visual field, which signals the superior colliculus to lower the sympathetic 'pressure' signal.

mind.text: The 50% Load. Place your hands flat on a wall or heavy desk. Push with 50% of your strength. Imagine the 'steam' moving from your chest, through your arms, and into the wood/stone.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing a sub-maximal 'vent' for motor energy. The external resistance proves you can move the load, which reclaim agency from the 'inside' pressure.

social.text: The Boundary Declaration. State out loud to the room: 'I am running high on energy right now. I need a 5-minute buffer.' Then physically take up more space.
social.physioTip: Best for: Exercising social agency to expand your 'personal space' perimeter, which tells the brain it is no longer being 'squeezed' by external demands.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's 'pressing' on you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Jaw Release. Open your mouth wide in a silent yawn. Use your fingertips to gently massage the hinges of your jaw. Let the tongue drop away from the roof of your mouth.
body.physioTip: Best for: Deactivating the 'Masseter-Temporal' clench. As the primary cranial bracing point, releasing here tells the brain it's safe to inhabit the 'midline' again.

mind.text: The Balloon Nozzle. Close your eyes. Imagine the pressure is a balloon. With every exhale, visualize a steady, narrow stream of air escaping from a nozzle in your chest.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using visceral imagery to facilitate a slow, controlled internal discharge of sympathetic energy, preventing a sudden reactive 'snap'.

social.text: The Vocal Hiss. Take a deep inhale through the nose. Exhale through your teeth with a long, steady 'Ssssss' sound. Focus entirely on the vibration in your teeth.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a mechanical and auditory 'vent' that mimics a physical pressure valve. It pulls the spaced-out brain back into the internal rhythm of the breath.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Vibrating
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel like you are vibrating. Your system is 'idling' at a high RPM, ready to move but waiting for a clear direction.
sensationData.blueprint: Preparatory Sympathetic Arousal. Your motor neurons are firing at a high frequency to prepare your muscles for action, even if you are standing still.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A 'humming' or 'buzzing' feeling in the hands and feet; a butterfly sensation in the stomach; and an inability to sit still.
sensationData.metaphor: A beehive in the summer—constant, high-frequency movement that sounds like a single, steady note.
sensationData.theImpulse: To fidget, to check your phone repeatedly, and to talk faster than usual.
sensationData.superpower: Alert Readiness. You are highly 'online'. Your reaction time is currently at its peak.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'hum' feels anxious internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: External Entrainment. Find a steady physical rhythm in the room (a ticking clock, a humming fan, or a bird chirping). Tap your finger to that external beat.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'motor entrainment' to pull awareness out of the internal 'vibration' and tether the nervous system to a predictable external pace.

mind.text: Texture Labeling. Pick one object in the room. Describe its texture in your head using 5 specific sensory words (e.g., 'gritty', 'cool', 'slick', 'woven', 'dense').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the associative cortex to shift the brain from high-speed 'threat scanning' to slow-speed 'evaluation,' pulling focus into the room.

social.text: The Buzz Ping. Text a safe friend a 'bee' emoji 🐝. If alone, say out loud: 'I am buzzing right now, I need to ground.' Listen to the sound of your voice.
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing the internal state to reduce the 'social masking' tax. Labeling the 'buzz' to the environment tells the brain the secret is out and it can settle.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels a bit blurry or too fast. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Butterfly Hug. Cross your arms over your chest. Slowly tap your shoulders, alternating left and right, like a slow metronome. Feel the touch on your skin.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using bilateral alternating stimulation to pull a 'spaced-out' brain back into its midline and organize sympathetic 'noise' into a coherent signal.

mind.text: The Internal Hum. Hum a steady, low note. Focus entirely on the physical vibration in your throat and chest. Feel the 'hum' moving your own ribs.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Stimulating the laryngeal branch of the vagus nerve to provide an internal 'anchor' that matches and then settles the vibrating sensation.

social.text: Prosody Focus. If someone is talking, ignore their words. Focus entirely on the melodic 'rise and fall' of their voice. Feel it as a wave of sound.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing vocal prosody to signal the ventral vagal system that the environment is safe, pulling the 'flickering' focus back into a state of presence.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Fidgety
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel fidgety. You have a surplus of 'mobilization' energy and your system is trying to find a productive way to use it.
sensationData.blueprint: Low-level Sympathetic Discharge. Your body is using 'micro-movements' (fidgeting) to bleed off excess adrenaline and keep the system from becoming overloaded.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Bouncing legs; tapping fingers; playing with hair or jewelry; and a constant need to shift your posture.
sensationData.metaphor: A puppy waiting by the door with a leash in its mouth. Ready to go, eager, and full of undirected movement.
sensationData.theImpulse: To start three different tasks at once, to clean something, or to pace while thinking.
sensationData.superpower: Active Creativity. This restlessness is the 'itch' that leads to discovery and getting things done.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel an internal 'itch' to move. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Resistance Game. If with someone, engage in a quick thumb-war or a brief 'palm-push' match. If alone, press your palms together firmly for 10 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using high-contrast physical resistance to provide an immediate external anchor, discharging restless energy through interaction with the environment.

mind.text: The 2-Minute Triage. Identify 3 tiny physical tasks you can see in the room (e.g., move a glass, straighten a pillow, close a drawer). Do ONE right now.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Channeling restless motor energy into a 'completion loop,' which provides an external dopamine hit and anchors the brain in the room.

social.text: The Micro-Service. Complete one tiny physical task that benefits the space or someone else (e.g., tidying a shared surface or refilling a water bottle).
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'tend-and-befriend' circuit to shift away from internal jittery arousal toward external social regulation.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Everything feels like a distraction. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Extremity Shake. Shake your hands and feet vigorously for 10 seconds. Stop abruptly and focus entirely on the 'tingle' of the blood returning to your fingers.
body.physioTip: Best for: Discharging sympathetic build-up and then immediately pulling focus back to the skin's surface and muscle spindles to re-occupy the body.

mind.text: The Texture Map. Close your eyes. Focus on the sensation of your clothes against your skin. Move slightly and identify if the texture is rough, smooth, or heavy.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from outward-focused distraction to inward-focused tactile grounding, pulling the 'spaced-out' brain back into the somatic envelope.

social.text: Object Manipulation. Find a 'fidget' object—a pen to click, a stone to rub, or a piece of fabric. Focus entirely on the internal resistance of the object against your fingers.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a continuous sensory-motor loop that occupies the 'excess' motor drive, anchoring the self back to the hands.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Pounding
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel a pounding sensation. Your heart is increasing its tempo to prepare your muscles for action. Even if you aren't moving, your body is ready to protect you.
sensationData.blueprint: Cardiovascular Activation. Your sympathetic nervous system has increased your heart rate and stroke volume, making the pulse feel more prominent in your chest, neck, or ears.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A rhythmic thumping in the chest; a pulsing feeling in the temples or throat; and a sense of internal 'drums' beating faster than the environment.
sensationData.metaphor: An engine idling at a high speed, ready for the race to start. The vibration is felt through the whole frame of the car.
sensationData.theImpulse: To check your pulse, to breathe rapidly, and to look for an immediate way to 'quiet' the noise in your chest.
sensationData.superpower: Circulatory Power. Your body is efficiently moving oxygen and fuel exactly where it needs to go. You are physically primed for high performance.
sensationData.generalGoal: Steadying the rhythm. Externalizing the beat.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The internal 'thump' is making you feel more anxious. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Square Tracing. Find 5 rectangular objects in the room (a window, a book, a screen). Slowly trace their four corners with your eyes, counting 1-2-3-4 for each.
body.physioTip: Best for: Engaging the oculomotor muscles in a structured geometric pattern to 'mark' the external environment, pulling focus away from internal cardiac noise.

mind.text: Serial 7s. Count backward from 100 by subtracting 7 each time (100, 93, 86...). Focus entirely on the math and say the numbers out loud.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the brain to exit a 'limbic hijack' by engaging the prefrontal cortex in a high-load analytical task, overriding internal panic signals.

social.text: Prosody Anchor. If someone is speaking, ignore their words. Focus entirely on the melodic 'rise and fall' of their voice. Use it to tether yourself to the room.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing external co-regulation to synchronize your internal tempo with a stable social signal, anchoring you in the shared space.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels too loud or fast for your heartbeat. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Baroreceptor Reset. Inhale through the nose for 4 seconds, then exhale through pursed lips (like a straw) for a slow 8 seconds. Feel the pressure change in your chest.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using PEEP (Positive End-Expiratory Pressure) to stimulate the baroreceptors and the vagus nerve, mechanically forcing the heart rate to drop from within.

mind.text: Rhythmic Entrainment. Tap a slow, steady beat on your own thigh. Focus on the physical contact of your hand against your leg. Make the tap the dominant rhythm.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using 'motor-pacing' to provide the brain with a competing, intentional tactile rhythm that overrides the involuntary pounding of the pulse.

social.text: The Heart Acknowledgment. Place your own hand firmly over your heart. Say silently: 'I hear you, heart. Thank you for the energy. We are inside, we are safe.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'self-affiliation' and tactile containment to reduce the fear-response to internal sensations, pulling awareness back to the somatic midline.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Guarded
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel guarded. Your system is protecting your softest parts because it's not yet sure if the current environment is fully safe.
sensationData.blueprint: Defensive Armoring. A state of mild sympathetic arousal where the muscles of the core, jaw, and shoulders slightly contract to protect vital areas from potential impact.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Shoulders slightly raised; jaw held tight; and a subtle 'bracing' in the belly. You feel like you are wearing an invisible suit of armor.
sensationData.metaphor: A drawbridge that has been raised halfway. You aren't closed off, but you are carefully controlling who and what gets in.
sensationData.theImpulse: To cross your arms, to weigh your words carefully, and to monitor the movements of others in the room.
sensationData.superpower: Strategic Discernment. You are not being 'cold'; you are being wise. You are curating your vulnerability until safety is proven.
sensationData.generalGoal: Testing the perimeter. Softening the edges.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel tight and 'walled in' internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The 180-Degree Scan. Slowly turn your head to the far left and far right. Notice 3 neutral objects (e.g., a door handle, a chair leg) that are definitively unmoving.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using cervical rotation to signal the superior colliculus that the periphery is clear, allowing the brain to release 'braced' postural muscles.

mind.text: Detail Appreciation. Pick one interesting object in the room. Silently list 3 precise details (e.g., 'the grain of the wood', 'the way light hits the edge').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'broad threat-scanning' to 'narrow detailed observation,' which forces the amygdala to yield to the prefrontal cortex.

social.text: The Half-Smile (Vagal Ping). Look at a neutral object and give it a tiny, almost invisible 'half-smile'. Notice the slight release in your jaw and around your eyes.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the facial feedback loop to trigger the ventral vagus, signaling the brain that the environment is safe enough for social engagement.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels like it's watching you. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Lateral Costal Breathing. Place your hands on the sides of your ribcage. Inhale and try to push your hands out to the sides with your ribs. Feel the expansion.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rib expansion to create internal space without the 'vulnerability' of a full belly breath, softening the intercostal tension from the inside out.

mind.text: The Safe Haven. Close your eyes for 5 seconds. Imagine a specific place where you feel 100% safe. Visualize the colors, the ground beneath you, and the temperature.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using mental architecture to provide the 'safety data' that the current environment is failing to deliver, pulling awareness back to a centered self.

social.text: The Protective Rub. Gently rub your own upper arms or shoulders with your palms. Focus entirely on the warmth and the texture of your own skin.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing 'self-soothing' tactile input to lower the guarding reflex in the trapezius, re-establishing your body as a safe place to be.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Scanning
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you are scanning. Your brain is trying to predict and prevent problems by looking for any small cue of trouble. Your 'radar' is just working overtime.
sensationData.blueprint: Hyper-Vigilant Orienting. The nervous system has narrowed your visual and auditory focus to detect threats, often leading to 'tunnel vision' or an inability to rest the eyes.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Eyes darting from object to object; a feeling of 'lightness' in the head; and a neck that feels slightly stiff from constant movement.
sensationData.metaphor: A lighthouse beam swinging rapidly across the water, searching for a rock that might not even be there.
sensationData.theImpulse: To check your phone, to look at the door, and to mentally rehearse 'what-if' scenarios over and over.
sensationData.superpower: High-Speed Pattern Recognition. You are currently a genius at noticing details. Your system is protecting your future by analyzing the present.
sensationData.generalGoal: Grounding the gaze. Broadening the view.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: Your thoughts are scanning for 'what-ifs'. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Panoramic Vision. Soften your gaze until you can see the far-left and far-right edges of the room at the same time without moving your head.
body.physioTip: Best for: Switching from foveal vision (sympathetic) to peripheral vision (parasympathetic), which sends a hardware signal to the brainstem to inhibit the 'threat-scan' and startle response.

mind.text: Targeted Categorization. Find 3 green things, 3 red things, and 3 yellow things in the room. Say their names silently as you find them.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Giving the 'scanning' reflex a specific, non-threatening analytical job to do, which re-engages the prefrontal cortex and anchors awareness in the room.

social.text: Neutral Fixation. Pick one non-human, neutral object (like a light switch or door handle). Look at it steadily for 10 seconds without letting your eyes jump.
social.physioTip: Best for: Practicing 'visual stillness' to prove to the nervous system that you can stop the environmental scan safely, breaking the worry-loop.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: Your eyes can't stop moving around the room. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Hallux Grounding. Press your big toes (hallux) firmly into the floor. Feel the solid, undeniable resistance of the ground beneath your feet.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using a distal, high-contrast pressure point to pull a 'light-headed' or spaced-out brain back down into the somatic container.

mind.text: The Evidence Check. Take one specific 'what-if' and ask: 'Is there physical, sensory proof for this in my body or this room right now?'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'predictive coding' loop by demanding real-time interoceptive data over future-based simulations, pulling focus back to the self.

social.text: The Verbal Anchor. Say out loud in a clear whisper: 'I am here, and I am currently [state your action, e.g., sitting]'. Feel the vibration in your throat.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using your own voice to create an 'auditory boundary' that pulls consciousness back into the midline.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Flushed
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel flushed. Your system is sending blood to your skin and face as part of a temperature-regulating and emotional response. It’s a visible sign of your internal heat.
sensationData.blueprint: Vasodilation. Sympathetic arousal causes the blood vessels near the surface of the skin to widen, often as a reaction to social stress or rising anger.
sensationData.physicalSensation: Heat in the cheeks, ears, or chest; a 'tingly' or 'prickly' feeling in the face; and a sense that the room has suddenly become warmer.
sensationData.metaphor: A boiler that has been turned up just one notch too high—not exploding, but the pipes are hot to the touch.
sensationData.theImpulse: To fan yourself, to look away, to splash water on your face, and to hide the visible 'redness'.
sensationData.superpower: Honest Expression. Your body is telling the truth. Being flushed is a powerful, non-verbal signal that a boundary has been touched.
sensationData.generalGoal: Cooling the current. Soothing the surface.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the heat rising within. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Radial Sink. Run cold water over your inner wrists for 30 seconds. Focus entirely on the temperature shift in your pulse points.
body.physioTip: Best for: Cooling the blood at a high-circulation point (radial artery) to help pull the systemic thermal energy out of the core and face.

mind.text: The Horizon Anchor. Look out a window at the furthest point possible. Notice the vast amount of space between your eyes and the horizon.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Expanding the visual field to break the 'social-exposure' tunnel vision, signaling the hypothalamus to settle the systemic heat.

social.text: The Buffer Step. Physically move your body back or away by 2 feet. Acknowledge the extra 'personal space' you have just created around you.
social.physioTip: Best for: Adjusting the personal-space perimeter to reduce the 'proximity alert' in the brain that often maintains a facial flush.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels too 'close' or irritating. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Sitali Cooling. Curl your tongue (or purse lips) and inhale slowly through the opening. Feel the cold air on your tongue; exhale through the nose.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing evaporative cooling on the tongue to send an immediate internal 'temperature drop' signal to the brainstem and hypothalamus.

mind.text: Blue Submersion. Close your eyes. Visualize a cool, calm pool of deep blue water. Imagine the heat leaving your face and chest as you dip your core into it.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using color-thermal association to prime the nervous system for vasoconstriction, pulling awareness back to a centered, cool self.

social.text: Thermal Conductance. Place the backs of your hands (usually the coolest part of your skin) firmly against your warm cheeks. Feel the heat transfer into your palms.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing an immediate, self-regulated 'heat sink' to inhabit your boundaries and reduce the feeling of social exposure.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Prickly
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel prickly. Your system is raising its 'quills' to keep people at a safe distance while you navigate a minor annoyance.
sensationData.blueprint: Mild Defensive Reactivity. A state where the threshold for 'irritation' is lowered, making neutral sounds or comments feel like sharp pokes.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A 'zinging' feeling in the skin; an urge to itch or twitch; and a general feeling of being 'on edge' or easily poked.
sensationData.metaphor: A cactus in a busy room. You are well-protected, but anyone who gets too close might get a little sting.
sensationData.theImpulse: To make a sharp comment, to roll your eyes, to move away from touch, and to 'bristle' at questions.
sensationData.superpower: Sensory Sensitivity. You are noticing exactly what is not working in the environment. This is the first step toward refinement and change.
sensationData.generalGoal: Smoothing the quills. Softening the contact.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel 'sharp' and irritable inside. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: High-Contrast Softness. Find a soft fabric nearby (a scarf, a cushion, or even a soft part of your own clothing). Press your palms into it slowly and notice the total lack of 'edges' or sharpness.
body.physioTip: Best for: Providing the brain with 'non-threatening' external tactile data to counteract the internal feeling of being 'spiky', pulling awareness into a softer environment.

mind.text: Audio Softening. Close your eyes. Search for the quietest, softest, most distant sound in the room (e.g., a distant bird or a faint hum). Follow that specific sound for 15 seconds.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'startle-ready' scanning to 'selective' soft hearing, which helps settle the auditory startle reflex and anchors focus outside the irritated self.

social.text: The Boundary Buffer. State out loud or mentally: 'I am feeling a bit prickly right now, so I am going to expand my personal space by two feet.' Physically move slightly away from others.
social.physioTip: Best for: Proactively managing the social perimeter to prove to the nervous system that you have the agency to protect your own boundaries without needing to 'sting'.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels abrasive and 'poky'. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Proprioceptive Sweep. Use your right hand to firmly and slowly stroke down your left arm, from shoulder to fingertips. Feel the heavy pressure. Repeat 3 times, then switch sides.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using deep pressure (A-beta fiber input) to override the 'prickly' superficial sensory signals, pulling awareness back into the somatic container to reset the irritation threshold.

mind.text: Label the Poke. Identify the exact stimulus that triggered the prickliness. Say silently: '[X] was the poke. That was out there, but this is just my system reacting in here.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Differentiating between the external trigger and the internal sensation, which re-engages the prefrontal cortex and anchors you back in your own 'center'.

social.text: The Hedgehog Signal. Text a safe person a hedgehog emoji 🦔. If alone, look at your own palms and say: 'I’m feeling spiky, and that’s okay. I’m staying inside my shell for a moment.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing a playful social anchor to inhabit the state, reducing the metabolic drain of 'social masking' and reclaiming your internal safety.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Agitated
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel agitated. Your system has detected something 'off' or repulsive in your environment and is providing a low-dose spark to help you push it away or create distance.
sensationData.blueprint: Boundary Alert. A mild sympathetic rise designed to increase your discernment and prompt you to move away from a stimulus that feels biologically or emotionally 'wrong'.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A slight 'itch' in the skin; a desire to wipe your hands; or a subtle tightening of the upper lip and nose.
sensationData.metaphor: Static electricity on a sweater; it’s not a shock, but it’s constant, annoying, and makes you want to shake it off.
sensationData.theImpulse: To move away, to change the subject, or to physically 'shake' your body to clear the sensation.
sensationData.superpower: Biological Integrity. You have a very fine-tuned 'disgust' response that protects you from things that aren't good for your system.
sensationData.generalGoal: Clearing the field. Changing the input.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel an internal 'ick' or agitation. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Sensory Reset. Physically change your immediate environment. Move to a different room, step onto a different floor texture, or open a window to change the air flow.
body.physioTip: Best for: Breaking the 'sensory lock' of an abrasive environment by providing the brain with fresh, neutral external data to overwrite the internal agitation.

mind.text: Olfactory Anchor. Seek out a strong, neutral or pleasant scent (e.g., coffee beans, a cut lemon, or fresh peppermint). Focus entirely on the inhale.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the olfactory bulb's direct pathway to the limbic system to rapidly 're-code' a disgust response, pulling focus into the room.

social.text: The Neutral Exit. Practice a low-stakes boundary statement out loud: 'I’m going to step out for a minute for some fresh air.' Then physically execute the move.
social.physioTip: Best for: Reclaiming social agency by asserting a simple, non-negotiable movement through space, proving to your system that you can exit the 'repulsive' zone.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels abrasive or 'sticky'. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Distal Flick. Shake your hands vigorously as if flicking water off your fingertips for 15 seconds. Then, press your palms together firmly and feel the stillness.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rapid rhythmic movement to discharge the 'skin-crawling' sensation, followed by isometric pressure to pull awareness back to the midline.

mind.text: Label the Boundary. Identify the sensation and say silently: 'My system is detecting an mismatch. This feeling is my body protecting my borders. I am in control of my skin.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Shifting from 'feeling gross' to 'observing a biological signal,' which re-engages the prefrontal cortex and anchors you back in your center.

social.text: The Boundary Ping. Send a simple 'exit' or 'shield' emoji to a safe person. If alone, look at your own hands and acknowledge: 'Safety is inside here; the mess is out there.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing a boundary with zero verbal load, which protects the social engagement system while you re-establish your internal 'container'.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Turbulent
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel turbulent. Your internal 'weather' is changing, and your system is trying to stay balanced while the waves get a little choppier.
sensationData.blueprint: Mild Sympathetic Instability. Your nervous system is fluctuating between 'seeking safety' and 'preparing for trouble', creating an uneven internal rhythm.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A 'swirling' feeling in the stomach; uneven breath; and a sense that your internal center of gravity is shifting.
sensationData.metaphor: An airplane flying through a light cloud bank; there are occasional bumps, but the wings are still steady.
sensationData.theImpulse: To fix something, to seek reassurance, or to keep moving so you don't have to feel the 'swirl'.
sensationData.superpower: Navigational Awareness. You are sensitive to subtle changes in the 'currents' around you, allowing you to adjust your course early.
sensationData.generalGoal: Stabilizing the hull. Finding the level.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel 'unsteady' and troubled internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Horizontal Anchor. Find a long, steady horizontal line in the room (the floor, a shelf, the horizon). Follow it slowly with your eyes from left to right and back again 3 times.
body.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the vestibulo-ocular reflex to re-establish a sense of a stable 'horizon' in the brain, pulling focus out of the internal sensation of unsteadiness.

mind.text: Stillness Search. Identify 3 things in your immediate environment that are completely still and physically unmoving (e.g., a heavy table, a wall, a lamp).
mind.physioTip: Best for: Providing the brain with 'fixed-point' data to override the internal sensation of swirling, anchoring the mind in objective external stability.

social.text: The Grounding Word. If with someone, ask for a single 'grounding' word. If alone, look at a solid object and say a word that represents 'stillness' out loud.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing an external auditory signal or co-regulation to provide a 'stable post' to hold onto, pulling the focus away from the internal turbulence.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels unpredictable. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Box Breathing. Inhale for 4, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4. Focus entirely on the physical sensation of the air and the 'hold' at the bottom.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using rhythmic holds to stabilize the heart rate and provide a structured internal 'metronome' to pull the spaced-out brain back into the body.

mind.text: Storm Labeling. Identify the one specific thing making the internal waves choppy. Name it silently, then visualize setting it on a shelf inside your mind for 5 minutes.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Reducing cognitive 'drag' by isolating the primary stressor, allowing the prefrontal cortex to regain control of the internal state.

social.text: Solar Plexus Grounding. Place your hand firmly on your solar plexus (just below the ribs). Feel the warmth of your palm and say silently: 'I am here, and I am steady.'
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a tactile anchor for the diaphragm, encouraging a lower center of gravity and pulling the awareness back into the physical midline.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Charged
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel charged. You have 'plugged in' to a concern, and your system is providing the electricity needed to pay attention and solve it.
sensationData.blueprint: Attentional Arousal. Your sympathetic system is providing a low-level 'spark' to keep your brain sharp and your body ready to act on a concern.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A light 'buzzing' in the forehead; ears that feel extra sensitive to sound; and a subtle 'ready' posture.
sensationData.metaphor: A battery that has just been placed on the charger; there is a steady flow of energy starting to build up.
sensationData.theImpulse: To ask questions, to double-check details, and to stay 'on alert' until the concern is resolved.
sensationData.superpower: Focused Vigilance. You are highly 'online' and capable of catching small errors or risks before they grow.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: The 'charge' is making your thoughts race. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Input Reduction. Lower the 'voltage' of the room right now: dim the lights, silence your phone notifications, or move to a quieter corner. Limit the incoming data.
body.physioTip: Best for: Manually lowering the amount of sensory data the 'Charged' brain has to process, preventing a spike into higher sympathetic arousal.

mind.text: Visual Dilation. Find the largest neutral surface in the room (a blank wall or the ceiling). Let your eyes rest there for 20 seconds without 'looking' for anything specific.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Switching off the foveal 'target-lock' visual mode that fuels hyper-vigilance, allowing the visual system to settle by looking 'out' at nothing.

social.text: The Concern Share. Tell a safe person: 'I am feeling a bit charged about [X].' If alone, say it out loud to the room. Just naming the electricity helps it move.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using 'affect labeling' to move the concern from the amygdala to the language centers, externalizing the internal charge into the social field.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The environment feels full of 'potential' problems. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Heel-Strike Grounding. If possible, take off your shoes. Press your heels firmly into the floor as if you are trying to leave an imprint. Hold for 5 seconds and release.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using high-pressure input at the base of the body to pull awareness back down from the 'head-heavy' attentional buzz and into the somatic midline.

mind.text: Gravitational Loading. Close your eyes for a moment. Focus entirely on the weight of your pelvis and thighs in the chair. Feel the downward pull of gravity holding you.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing 'heavy' sensory data from the lower body to counteract the 'light/buzzy' feeling of head-centered anxiety, anchoring you back in.

social.text: The Priority Filter. Identify the most urgent concern. Set a mental timer to think only about that ONE thing. Breathe and feel that specific focus in your chest.
social.physioTip: Best for: Reducing the 'processing tax' by collapsing multiple external alerts into a single internal stream, allowing the system to inhabit one point of focus.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Unsettled
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel unsettled. Your system is in a 'wait and see' mode, sensing that things aren't quite aligned, which creates a mild internal friction.
sensationData.blueprint: Low-grade Orienting Tension. Your nervous system is scanning for a missing piece of safety or information, keeping you in a state of 'unguarded' readiness.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A 'shifting' feeling in the chest; a need to move your feet; and eyes that won't settle on one point for very long.
sensationData.metaphor: Dust motes dancing in a beam of light—constantly moving, never quite landing, waiting for the air to go still.
sensationData.theImpulse: To pace, to adjust your clothing, and to 'look around' for the source of the unease.
sensationData.superpower: Intuitive Sensing. You are picking up on a subtle mismatch in your environment. Trust your system's ability to 'notice' the nuances.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel an internal 'shiftiness'. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: Tactile Discriminatory Scan. Reach out and touch 3 different textures in your immediate environment: something hard (desk), something soft (fabric), and something cold (metal). Identify the distinct difference in your mind.
body.physioTip: Best for: Shifting the brain from 'distal threat scanning' (eyes/ears) to 'proximal safety testing' (hands), pulling focus out of internal unease and into the physical room.

mind.text: The 20-Second Fixation. Pick a single, small, unmoving spot on the wall or a piece of furniture. Look at it steadily for 20 seconds without letting your eyes wander.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Manually inhibiting the superior colliculus's urge to 'scan,' proving to the brain that it is safe to remain still in this environment.

social.text: The Mundane Anchor. If with someone, ask: 'What’s on your schedule for tomorrow?' If alone, look at your own calendar. Focus entirely on practical, boring, external details.
social.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing social entrainment to shift the brain away from abstract internal unease toward concrete, external reality.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The room feels 'off'. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: Ischial Tuberosity Grounding. Sit firmly on a hard chair. Rock slightly until you feel the sharp 'sit-bones' in your pelvis, then settle your full weight directly onto them. Feel the vertical support.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using skeletal loading to provide a clear 'vertical anchor' for a nervous system that feels internally untethered, pulling focus back into the physical midline.

mind.text: The 'Right Now' List. Name 3 things that are biologically true for your body in this exact second (e.g., 'My feet are touching the floor', 'I can feel the air in my nostrils').
mind.physioTip: Best for: Interrupting the 'future-threat' scanning by forcing the brain to process immediate, non-negotiable internal sensory data.

social.text: Bilateral Humerus Compression. Cross your arms and give your own opposite upper arms (the humerus bone) a firm, steady squeeze. Hold for 10 seconds and feel your own boundaries.
social.physioTip: Best for: Providing a 'boundary' signal to the brain, using self-touch to activate the ventral vagal system and pull the 'spaced out' awareness back into the skin.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Tense
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel tense. You’ve encountered a small 'knot' in your day, and your muscles are tightening to help you pull through it.
sensationData.blueprint: Mild Sympathetic Tone. A slight increase in muscle spindles' sensitivity, preparing your body to exert force if the 'peeved' feeling escalates.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A tight jaw; a 'string' pulling between the shoulder blades; and a feeling of being 'wound up' like a spring.
sensationData.metaphor: A guitar string that has been tuned just a half-note too sharp. The sound is clear, but it feels like it might snap if pulled harder.
sensationData.theImpulse: To sigh with frustration, to move with 'sharp' gestures, and to be short with your answers.
sensationData.superpower: Ready-to-Act. This tension is 'work-ready' energy. It is the beginning of the focus needed to solve a problem or set a boundary.

pathA.title: Path A: Stuck Inside
pathA.goal: You feel the 'knot' and frustration internally. Goal: Pull OUT.

body.text: The Foveal Break. Look at the furthest point possible—out a window or down a long hallway. Soften your gaze until the edges of the room feel slightly blurry for 15 seconds.
body.physioTip: Best for: Inhibiting narrow foveal vision to shift the brain from a 'threat-fixated' sympathetic state to a 'global-scanning' parasympathetic state, pulling awareness out of the internal knot.

mind.text: Relaxation Mirroring. Identify one object in your environment that is 'limp' or 'draped' (e.g., a scarf on a chair, a hanging plant leaf, a sleeping pet). Look at it and imagine having its weight.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Using external visual 'anchors' of low-tone objects to prime the brain for its own muscular down-regulation by looking 'at' relaxation in the world.

social.text: Pharyngeal Reset. Perform a wide, exaggerated 'fake' yawn. Stretch the back of your throat and open your mouth as wide as possible, exhaling with a soft sound.
social.physioTip: Best for: Mechanically stretching the muscles of the jaw and pharynx to stimulate the glossopharyngeal nerve, triggering a reset that opens the internal 'clench' to the outside.

pathB.title: Path B: Stuck Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels annoying or high-pressure. Goal: Pull IN.

body.text: The Scapular Drop. Inhale deeply while pulling your shoulders toward your ears. Hold for 2 seconds, then exhale sharply and let your shoulders drop like heavy weights.
body.physioTip: Best for: Using brief, high-intensity contraction followed by a gravity-assisted release to reset the tone of the upper trapezius, pulling focus back to the physical midline.

mind.text: The Micro-Softening. Close your eyes. Focus only on the corners of your mouth. Soften them by 10% and imagine the space between your upper and lower molars widening.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Utilizing the facial feedback loop to send a 'low-threat' signal to the brainstem, reducing secondary jaw tension and anchoring you back in your skin.

social.text: The Sigh Signal. Text a 'sigh' emoji 😮‍💨 to a trusted friend or say 'sigh' out loud to yourself. Acknowledge your state without starting a complex conversation.
social.physioTip: Best for: Externalizing minor irritation to prevent it from 'stewing', using a simple social anchor to reclaim your internal space from environmental annoyance.

sensationData.warning: What do you want to achieve? Do you want to use the energy? Then steer to yellow. Do you want to relax? Calm down into green.

sensationData.title: Still
sensationData.validation: It makes sense that you feel still. Your system has reached a rare point of total equilibrium where no effort is required. You are safely anchored in the present moment.
sensationData.blueprint: Ventral Vagal dominance. Your parasympathetic system is in its most restorative 'rest and digest' mode. Heart rate and breathing are at their most efficient, rhythmic baseline.
sensationData.physicalSensation: A feeling of weightlessness or 'soft' boundaries; muscles are completely devoid of holding-tension; breath is deep, slow, and effortless.
sensationData.metaphor: A mountain lake at dawn—the water is so flat it acts as a perfect mirror, undisturbed by even a single ripple.
sensationData.theImpulse: To remain exactly as you are, to breathe deeply, and to simply 'be' without any need to do.
sensationData.superpower: Deep Restoration. In this state of total stillness, your body is performing its most vital cellular and emotional repair work.

pathA.title: Path A: Anchored Inside
pathA.goal: You are deeply submerged in internal peace. Goal: Train OUT.

body.text: Space-Time Bridging. Close your eyes for 3 breaths. Open them and focus on your palm for 3 breaths. Look 15 feet away for 3 breaths. Finally, look at the furthest horizon possible for 3 breaths.
body.physioTip: Best for: Exercising the neural flexibility to slide from internal peace to external engagement, preventing the brain from 'parking' too long in a purely internal state.

mind.text: The Detail Grid. Pick one object in the room. Mentally place a 3x3 grid over it and identify one specific texture or shadow in each of the 9 squares.
mind.physioTip: Best for: Forcing the foveal visual system to perform high-detail external work, which 'cross-trains' the brain while in a relaxed state.

social.text: Environmental Gratitude. Whisper a 'thank you' to your current surroundings. Identify one physical part of the room that is actively supporting your peace right now.
social.physioTip: Best for: Using the 'Mind Lever' to link your internal serenity to the external environment, reinforcing the world as a safe co-regulation partner.

pathB.title: Path B: Anchored Outside
pathB.goal: The world feels distant and non-threatening. Goal: Train IN.

body.text: Neutral Point Scanning. Scan your body for the most 'quiet' spot (e.g., your earlobes, the back of your knees, or the space between your toes). Simply rest your attention there.
body.physioTip: Best for: Reinforcing the brain's internal map of 'safety-points' (The Body Lever), which builds a somatic library you can return to when in high-stress states.

mind.text: Archetype Integration: The Witness. Say silently: 'As the Witness, I observe the rise and fall of my own breath. I am the space through which the air moves.'
mind.physioTip: Best for: Building metacognitive capacity. It uses the Mind Lever to separate your identity from the physical sensation, strengthening your 'observing self'.
